questions.title: Animal Moral Weights
questions.heading: How much moral weight do you give to the welfare of animals compared to humans?
questions.info: Moral weights determine how much you value improvements to different types of beings. Human life-years are the baseline (1.0). Your answer here affects how the model scores funds that focus on animal welfare. Each option sets weights across species and effect types (life-years, disability, income doublings).

options.label: Only humans matter
options.description: Animal welfare is not counted. Only effects on humans are included in fund scores.

options.label: Animals matter, but much less than humans
options.description: Gives animals meaningful but low moral weight. Reflects substantial scepticism about the depth of animal experience relative to human experience.

options.label: RP weighted average
options.description: A weighted average across the moral weight estimates that Rethink Priorities researchers find most plausible, giving animals meaningful but not equal weight to humans.

options.label: All sentient beings count equally
options.description: A year of suffering counts the same regardless of species. Strong moral egalitarianism across all sentient life.

questions.title: Time Discounting
questions.heading: How much do you care about effects that happen in the future, compared to effects happening now?
questions.info: The model divides effects into six time periods: 0–5 years, 5–10 years, 10–20 years, 20–100 years, 100–500 years, and 500+ years. Your answer sets a multiplier for each period. A multiplier of 0 means effects in that period are ignored entirely; 1 means they count fully.

options.label: Near-term only (next 10 years)
options.description: Only effects occurring before 2035 count. Medium and long-term effects are ignored entirely.

options.label: Next 100 years
options.description: Near and medium-term effects count; effects after 2100 are ignored. Emphasises people alive today and those born in the next few generations.

options.label: More weight on near-term, some weight on long-term
options.description: Near-term effects count fully. Medium-term effects count somewhat. Long-term effects count a little but are substantially discounted. RP's recommended starting point.

options.label: All timeframes count equally
options.description: A good outcome in 500 years counts just as much as one today. Pure longtermist view with no time discounting.

questions.title: Non-AI X-Risk Discount
questions.heading: How much do you discount non-AI existential risk interventions, given the possibility that AI poses an overriding near-term threat?
questions.info: This adjustment applies only to non-AI x-risk interventions (e.g. biosecurity, pandemic prevention, nuclear risk reduction). The value is a discount fraction — 0.0 means no discount, 0.4 means a 40% discount, 1.0 means they score zero. AI safety and global health interventions are unaffected.

options.label: No discount
options.description: Non-AI existential risk interventions are evaluated the same way as all other interventions.

options.label: Small discount (10%)
options.description: Non-AI x-risk interventions are scored at 90% of their otherwise calculated value.

options.label: Large discount (40%)
options.description: Non-AI x-risk interventions are scored at 60% of their otherwise calculated value. RP's view that AI risk is a substantial near-term priority that partially crowds out other existential risk work.

options.label: Only AI risk matters (100% discount)
options.description: Non-AI existential risk interventions are scored at zero.

questions.title: Risk Attitude
questions.heading: How do you want to handle uncertainty and risk when comparing funds?
questions.info: Each fund's impact is estimated across a range of scenarios with different probabilities. The risk profile determines how those scenarios are weighted. A risk-neutral approach takes a straight expected value. WLU (Weighted Linear Utility) applies a formal variance-aversion method that penalises high-variance outcomes.

options.label: Risk neutral
options.description: Funds are scored using standard expected value. A 1% chance of 100x impact counts the same as a certain 1x impact.

options.label: Sceptical of very large upside scenarios
options.description: Discounts the most optimistic impact scenarios. Funds whose scores depend heavily on low-probability, very large outcomes will score lower.

options.label: Penalise risk of doing harm
options.description: Applies extra weight to scenarios where an intervention backfires or causes harm. Funds with significant downside risk will score lower even if their expected value is high.

options.label: Variance-averse (WLU)
options.description: Uses a formal variance-aversion method (Weighted Linear Utility) that penalises high-variance outcomes. RP's recommended approach for donors who care about robustness across scenarios.
